rulings.Case Title: CHIN-TEH SHIH et al VS. ERIC LIEN et al
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, November 5, 2013, Line 3, DEFENDANTs PI-CHING YEN and ERIC LIEN's Motion For Attorney's Fees. Granted. The requested rate of $400 per hour is reasonable. Mr. Walker has been the attorney representing Lien and Yen in this case for several years, including before he moved his office to Maryland. The fact that Mr. Walker requested $300 per hour five years ago in connection with attorney fees in another motion in another one of the cases between these parties does not preclude an award of $400 per hour in November 2013. The Court finds that the hours spent from May 10, 2013 through July 26, 2013 are reasonable hours spent for work on the successful anti-SLAPP motion that is the subject of this request for attorney fees. These hours total 72.6 hours. In addition, the Court finds that counsel should be awarded attorney fees for 5 hours spent in preparing this motion for fees. The Court does not award attorney fees for the first anti-SLAPP motion which was denied by the Court on May 28, 2013. The grounds for the first and second motions (and the oppositions thereto) were sufficiently different such that it would not be appropriate to award attorney fees for time spent on the unsuccessful motion. Attorney fees are awarded in favor of Defendants and against Plaintiffs in the amount of $31,040 to be paid within 30 days of service of notice of the entry of order. (CCP § 425.16(c).) Defendants' request for judicial notice is denied. The Court does not take judicial notice of unpublished decisions of the Court of Appeal or the contents of an order in an unrelated case. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/MJM)

rulings.Case Title: CAROL A. TRUFANT VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, November 5, 2013, Line 13, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO's MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS. Defendant's motion for judgment on the pleadings is granted with 10 days leave to amend. The record shows that Plaintiff failed to file a timely opposition to the motion. This is a basis to deny the motion. However, the Court will consider the brief and address the merits. Plaintiff has failed to allege a statutory basis for her claims against public entity Defendant. (See Eastburn v. Regional Fire Protection Authority (2003) 31 Cal.4th 1175, 1183; Zelig v. County of Los Angeles (2002) 27 Cal.4th 1112, 1132.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: DAVID HERNANDEZ VS. RT WESTERN MAINTENANCE, INC. et al
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, November 5, 2013, Line 16, DEFENDANT RT WESTERN MAINTENANCE, INC.'s DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. The demurrer is overruled. The Request for Judicial Notice is denied. Defendant asks the Court to take judicial notice of the "documents attached" as Exhibits A, B, and C to the request for judicial notice. A court may not take judicial notice of the contents of documents in a court or agency file. (Arce v. Kaiser Foundation Health Plan, Inc. (2010) 181 Cal. App. 4th 471, 482.) As to Exhibit C, it is unauthenticated and appears to be a letter or notice. In any event, even if the Court were to take judicial notice of Exhibit A, it merely establishes that a worker's compensation claim was filed on a certain date. This is an insufficient basis to sustain the demurrer, in light of the allegations in the Complaint. Further, Defendant's reply brief raises, for the first time, a new argument regarding the application of 33 U.S.C. §904 and §905(a) which will be not be considered as grounds for demurrer.
